summary: Sima Zhao; launches the conquest of Shu. Three-pronged assault: Zhong Hui (180,000) at Hanzhong by the main road; Deng Ai (30,000) at Tazhong to pin Jiang Wei; Zhuge Xu (30,000) at Wujie. The campaign that brings down the Han loyalist regime after sixty years.
Zhong Hui; commands the main 180,000-strong Wei thrust at Hanzhong. Takes Yangping Pass; advances on Sword Pass (Jianmen). The ambitious co-commander; will soon scheme against Deng Ai.
Deng Ai; commands the western prong against Jiang Wei at Tazhong; the strategist who will outflank Sword Pass via Yinping.
Zhuge Xu; commands the central column; gets blocked by Jiang Wei's manoeuvre to Sword Pass.
Jiang Wei; at Tazhong; intercepts the Wei thrust; falls back through Sword Pass to hold the Jianmen line. The chapter's chief Shu hero.

Huang Hao; the chapter's villain. With Jiang Wei in the field, Huang Hao consults a witch; the witch tells him to ignore the campaign. Reports from the frontier never reach Liu Shan. Shu drifts unprepared into the war.
Jiang Shu; in the Wei force.
Fu Qian; defends the Hanzhong pass; killed in the Wei breakthrough.
Liu Shi; Shu officer; defends the Hanzhong line.
Sima Wang; with the Wei force.
Xu Chu; named in passing - long dead.
Xiahou Yuan; named in passing in the historical recap.
Dong Jue; in Liu Shan's court.
Hu Lie; Wei officer; with the conquest force.
Lu Xu; in Liu Shan's court.
Wei Guan; Sima Zhao's high-ranking officer accompanying the campaign - the watcher who will later arrest Zhong Hui.
Zhuge Liang; named in passing - his spirit haunts Zhong Hui who visits his shrine on the road.

Deng Ai; the chapter's centerpiece. With Sword Pass impregnable from the front, takes the famously impossible road through the Yinping ravines: 700 li of pathless cliffs and gorges; his men wrap themselves in felt and roll down sheer drops; Deng Ai himself wraps in blankets and rolls. Emerges behind the Shu defences at Jiangyou which Ma Miao surrenders without a fight; then defeats Zhuge Zhan at Mianzhu. The ride that wins the war.

Zhuge Zhan; Zhuge Liang's son. Defends Mianzhu against Deng Ai's emergence from Yinping; refuses the offer of marquisate to surrender; charges out with his son Zhuge Shang at the head of his men and dies fighting. The honourable last gasp of Zhuge Liang's bloodline.
Zhuge Shang; Zhuge Zhan's son. Charges out with his father at Mianzhu; killed beside him. The grandson of the Sleeping Dragon dies sword in hand.
Ma Miao; Shu commander at Jiangyou; surrenders to Deng Ai without a fight when the Wei force erupts from Yinping. The opening gate.
Jiang Wei; holds Sword Pass against Zhong Hui - irrelevantly, since Deng Ai has gone around him. The chapter's tragic structural irony.
Zhong Hui; held in front of Sword Pass; sees Deng Ai's emergence as theft of his prize; jealousy begins. The first sparks of the dual-commander rivalry.
Sima Zhao; in the Wei capital.
Sun Xiu; Emperor of Wu; sends Ding Feng with a relief force toward Shu.
Ding Feng; commands the Wu relief column; reaches the frontier but Shu has already collapsed.
Huang Hao; still suppressing reports at Liu Shan's court; the rot extends.
Liao Hua; with Jiang Wei at Sword Pass.
Deng Zhong; Deng Ai's son; with the Yinping crossing.
Dong Jue; in Liu Shan's court.
Xi Zheng; in Liu Shan's court.
Liu Zhang; named in passing - long dead.
Zhang Fei; named in passing - long dead.
Zhang Ni; with the Shu force.
Zhang Yi; with the Shu force.
Zhang Zun; Shu officer; killed defending Mianzhu.
Wei Guan; with the Wei high command.
Zhuge Xu; punished by Sima Zhao for letting Jiang Wei slip past him.
Zhuge Liang; named in passing throughout the Zhuge-clan death sequence.

Liu Chan; Liu Shan's fifth son and the iconic refuser. Tries to stop the surrender; argues passionately for resistance; goes to his father's ancestral temple, weeps prostrate before Liu Bei's tablet, kills his consort and three sons in the temple, then cuts his own throat. The single noble act in the chapter's wreckage.
Lady Cui; Liu Chan's consort; killed by him before his suicide as he refuses surrender.

Qiao Zhou; the chapter's villain. The astrologer-courtier who has counseled Liu Shan to fear war for decades; now leads the surrender petition; produces every astrological and historical argument for capitulation. The Shu collapse from inside.
Deng Ai; accepts the surrender; treats Liu Shan correctly; appoints his own subordinates as Shu administrators in his own name - beginning the breach with Sima Zhao that will undo him in chapter 119.
Huang Hao; the eunuch survives the surrender; Deng Ai initially keeps him; Sima Zhao later has him executed for ruining Shu.

Jiang Wei; receives the news of Liu Shan's surrender while still holding Sword Pass. Tears off his armour and weeps; orders his men to surrender to Zhong Hui - but secretly plots with Zhong Hui to use him as the lever against Wei: 'if Wei falls, perhaps Shu rises again.'
Zhong Hui; receives the Sword Pass surrender; charmed by Jiang Wei; the two strike up the alliance that will become the chapter-119 rebellion against Wei.
Sima Zhao; in the capital; receives Deng Ai's reports of unauthorized appointments; sees the danger; will move to crush him.
Jia Chong; in the Wei court.
Wei Guan; will be the one tasked to arrest both Deng Ai and Zhong Hui.
Zhang Fei; named in passing - long dead.
Guan Yu; named in passing in the elegy.
Zhuge Zhan; named in passing - already dead.
Zhuge Shang; named in passing - already dead.
Zhuge Liang; named in passing in the dynastic elegy.

Zhong Hui dies. The chapter's centerpiece. Together with Jiang Wei, plots open rebellion against Sima Zhao at Chengdu - forges Empress Guo's decree to depose Sima Zhao; declares himself King of Yizhou; tries to imprison the Wei officers who refuse to follow. They mutiny; Zhong Hui and Jiang Wei are both cut down in the palace fighting. The Wei conquest commanders consume each other; Shu remains in Wei hands.

Jiang Wei dies. Pushed Zhong Hui into rebellion as his lever for a last Shu restoration. When the mutiny breaks Jiang Wei tries to break out; struck down; his death cry: 'My plan failed because of Heaven, not for lack of skill.' Cuts his own throat. The cause Zhuge Liang inherited from Liu Bei finally dies with him.

Deng Ai dies. Arrested for his unauthorized Shu appointments. While being transported to Luoyang in a prison cage, Wei Guan and Tian Xu - fearing his exoneration - overtake the cart and murder him and his son on the road.
Deng Zhong; Deng Ai's son; killed beside his father on the road.
Wei Guan; directs the arrests of Deng Ai and Zhong Hui; orders Deng Ai's killing on the road; survives to high office.
Tian Xu; the officer Wei Guan dispatches to murder Deng Ai en route.
Xi Zheng; the courtier who tries to coach Liu Shan toward dignity at the Luoyang banquet; backfires when Liu Shan parrots him by name.

Sima Zhao dies. With Shu conquered and Wei back under control, Sima Zhao approaches the title of Duke of Jin; on the verge of accepting the imperial throne. Dies before formally accepting. Sima Yan succeeds.
Sima Yan; succeeds his father Sima Zhao as Duke of Jin. Will depose Cao Huan and take the imperial throne next chapter.
Empress Guo; her name and decree are forged by Zhong Hui to legitimise the rebellion.
Jia Chong; in the Wei court.
Pei Xiu; in the Wei court.
Wang Su; in the Wei court.
Liao Hua; with Jiang Wei at Chengdu; dies of grief shortly after Jiang Wei's death.
Huang Hao; executed by Sima Zhao for his role in Shu's collapse.
Qiao Zhou; transferred to Luoyang with Liu Shan.
Fan Jian; in the old Shu court.
Dong Jue; in the old Shu court.
Sima Fu; in the Wei court.
Sima Liang; in the Wei court.
Sima Shi; named in passing - long dead.
Sima Wang; in the Wei court.
Sima Yi; named in passing - long dead.
Sima You; Sima Zhao's second son.
Cao Cao; named in passing in the elegy.
Guan Yi; Guan Yu's grandson; named in passing.
He Zeng; in the Wei court.
Hu Lie; Wei officer killed in the Chengdu mutiny.
Lu Kang; Lu Xun's son; first major appearance - will be the great Wu commander of the final decades.
Lu Xun; named in passing - long dead.
Shan Tao; in the Wei court.
Zhang Liang; named in passing - Han-founding allusion.
Zhuge Liang; named throughout in the final elegies - the spirit that haunts the unmade Shu restoration.

Sima Yan; founds the Jin dynasty. Deposes Cao Huan; takes the imperial throne; era Taishi year 1. Then mounts the final conquest of Wu - the great river campaign with Wang Jun's chained-link warship fleet sailing down from Sichuan and Yang Hu / Du Yu coordinating the eastern push. Wu surrenders in 280 CE; Sima Yan unites all of China. The novel closes.

Sun Hao; the last emperor of Wu. The chapter's tyrant. Cruel, capricious, executes ministers wholesale (Lu Kai, Wan Yu, He Ding); flays faces, gouges eyes. Drives the kingdom to ruin. Eventually surrenders to Jin and is treated kindly in Luoyang - survives in exile.

Lu Kang; Lu Xun's son and the great Wu commander of the final years. Holds Jingzhou for Wu through Yang Hu's gentlemanly stand-off - the two commanders trade medicine and wine in chivalric exchange across the river. Lu Kang dies of illness; Wu's last defender gone; the final Jin invasion follows immediately.

Yang Hu; the Wei/Jin commander opposite Lu Kang. The famous gentlemanly stand-off across the Wu-Jin frontier. Recommends Du Yu and Wang Jun to Sima Yan as the right men for the final campaign. Dies before the conquest is complete.
Du Yu; the strategic commander who finalises the Wu conquest with Yang Hu's plan.

Wang Jun; the chapter's most iconic figure on the Jin side. Designs the great chained warship fleet built up the Min and Han rivers in Sichuan; sails down the Yangtze breaking every Wu river-barrier; takes Jianye (Wu capital). The river fleet that ends Three Kingdoms.
Wang Hun; co-commander on the eastern Yangtze axis; arguments with Wang Jun over precedence.
Sun Xiu dies. The Wu emperor; dies before the chapter's end; Sun Hao succeeds him to the Wu throne.
Ding Feng dies. The last great Wu officer; dies in this chapter of old age. The Sun-clan's last institutional bulwark gone.
Jia Chong; in the Jin court.
Jiang Wei; named in passing - dead.
Deng Ai; named in passing - dead.
Zhong Hui; named in passing - dead.
Liu Shan; the chapter's final image. Dies in Luoyang in luxurious obscurity, aged 65, the Marquis of Anle.
Huang Hao; named in passing - executed.
Liu Bei; named in passing in the closing elegies.
Cao Cao; named in passing in the closing elegies.
Sun Quan; named in passing in the closing elegies.
Sun Jian; named in passing - founding ancestor.
Bu Chan; Wu defender; surrenders to Jin.
Cen Hun; one of Sun Hao's worst counsellors.
Dong Zhuo; named in passing in the closing historical elegies.
Gongsun Zan; named in passing in the closing historical elegies.
Liu Zhang; named in passing - long dead.
Sima Zhou; in the Jin court.
Wan Yu; Wu official; killed by Sun Hao.
Wang Yun; named in passing - long dead.
Xun Xu; in the Jin court.
Zhang Hua; in the Jin court.

Zhuge Liang; named in passing in the closing elegy that ends the novel: 'Of the three contending heroes only their names remain; rivers and hills as before, but the kingdoms gone like a passing dream.'
